blocks.text: PLANTAE - TRACHEOPHYTA - MAGNOLIOPSIDA - DIPSACALES - VIBURNACEAE - Viburnum - erubescens

blocks.text:
Common Names: Reddish Viburnum (English)
Synonyms: Solenotinus erubescens (Wall.) Oerst.; Viburnum thibeticum C.Y.Wu & Y.F.Huang; Viburnum wightianum Wall.; Viburnum erubescens var. gracilipes Rehder; Viburnum erubescens var. parvum P.S.Hsu & S.C.Hsu; Viburnum prattii Graebn.; Viburnum pubigerum Wight & Arn.; Viburnum burmanicum var. motoense P.S.Hsu; Viburnum botryoideum H.Lév.; Viburnum burmanicum (Rehder) C.Y.Wu ex P.S.Hsu
blocks.text_rich:
Common Names: Reddish Viburnum (English)
Synonyms: Solenotinus erubescens (Wall.) Oerst.; Viburnum thibeticum C.Y.Wu & Y.F.Huang; Viburnum wightianum Wall.; Viburnum erubescens var. gracilipes Rehder; Viburnum erubescens var. parvum P.S.Hsu & S.C.Hsu; Viburnum prattii Graebn.; Viburnum pubigerum Wight & Arn.; Viburnum burmanicum var. motoense P.S.Hsu; Viburnum botryoideum H.Lév.; Viburnum burmanicum (Rehder) C.Y.Wu ex P.S.Hsu

blocks.text:
Taxonomic Note: 
This taxon shows a complex variation pattern. The number of flowers and number of lateral veins has been used to recognize separate taxa such as Viburnum burmanicum, V. erubescens var. parvum and V. prattii (Wu et al. 2011).
blocks.text_rich:
Taxonomic Note: 
This taxon shows a complex variation pattern. The number of flowers and number of lateral veins has been used to recognize separate taxa such as Viburnum burmanicum, V. erubescens var. parvum and V. prattii (Wu et al. 2011).

blocks.text: This plant species occupies a widespread distribution from the Himalayas to central China, , Viet Nam, India, and Sri Lanka. It has a large extent of occurrence (EOO) of 6,606,089 km2 , which exceeds the threshold value for threatened categories. There is limited population information available. Given its large native range, it is suspected that the global population is large and stable. The population is not known to be severely fragmented, and it is not known to be subject to extreme fluctuations. It is therefore assessed as Least Concern (LC). More research is needed on population status and trends, and the severity of threats to this plant in the future.

More research is needed on population status and trends, and the severity of threats to this plant in the future.

blocks.text: This plant occupies a widespread distribution from the Himalaya to central China, Viet Nam, south India, and Sri Lanka (POWO 2022). This species usually grows at elevations between 1,402-3,962 m asl.

blocks.text: The global population size of this species is not known but, as it is very widespread, it is assumed that the global population is large. Due to a lack of identified threats to this species, it is suspected that the global population trend is stable. Further research is needed to confirm the population status and trend.

blocks.text: Current Population Trend: Stable

blocks.text: This species is a deciduous shrub or small tree up to 1.5-6 m, distinguished by inflorescences in drooping panicles. It is found in occurs in forests and shrubberies (Polunin et al. 1984), particularly broad-leaved conifer-rhododendron and evergreen oak forest, and sometimes in disturbed areas (Grierson and Long 2001,. It is reported to flower from April to May.

It is found in occurs in forests and shrubberies (Polunin et al. 1984), particularly broad-leaved conifer-rhododendron and evergreen oak forest, and sometimes in disturbed areas (Grierson and Long 2001,. It is reported to flower from April to May.

blocks.text_rich: System: Terrestrial

blocks.text: Wild relative of a crop? No
blocks.text_rich: Wild relative of a crop? No

blocks.text: In some parts of India, the leave of this species is used as an insecticide (Singh et al. 2019, Rad et al. 2021). The leaves and stems of the species have been used for pharmacognostic investigation with isolated compounds including phytosterols, glycosides, and phenolic compounds such as flavonoids and procyanidins. (Prabhu et al. 2009). The roots, stems, and leaves of this species are also used for cough treatment (Rad et al. 2021). This plant can be used as fuel wood and for landscaping purposes.
blocks.text_rich: In some parts of India, the leave of this species is used as an insecticide (Singh et al. 2019, Rad et al. 2021). The leaves and stems of the species have been used for pharmacognostic investigation with isolated compounds including phytosterols, glycosides, and phenolic compounds such as flavonoids and procyanidins. (Prabhu et al. 2009). The roots, stems, and leaves of this species are also used for cough treatment (Rad et al. 2021). This plant can be used as fuel wood and for landscaping purposes.

blocks.text: This plant species had ethno-medicinal importance and has been used for pharmacognostic investigation, however, the extent of its usage and impact on the population of the species is currently unknown. Overexploitation of the species for these purposes may represent a minor threat to the species currently, while overcollection of plants for landscaping might pose a threat to the species in future.

blocks.text: This plant species is known to be represented in ex situ conservation in botanical gardens (BGCI 2022). This species is found and recorded in Bhutan within the protected areas and nature reserves. In some plants of India, this species occurs in sacred forests.
